title: Dog Thick Green Eye Discharge: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Learn what thick green eye discharge in dogs means, why it is a medium-priority concern, and how photos can help your vet triage the issue fast.
content_html:
TL;DR: Thick green eye discharge in dogs indicates an infection or severe irritation that requires a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours, or immediately if your dog is squinting or in pain.

What does it mean if my dog has thick green discharge coming from their eye?

Thick green discharge, medically known as mucopurulent discharge, is a sign that your dog's immune system is actively fighting an infection or severe irritation. Unlike clear tears, which are often related to allergies or dust, green or yellow mucus contains white blood cells and debris. This is frequently seen with conditions such as bacterial conjunctivitis, corneal ulcers, or Keratoconjunctivitis Sicca (dry eye).

Is thick green eye discharge in dogs a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: This condition is classified as a Medium Urgency, meaning your dog should be seen by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours.
  • When to Seek Immediate Care: If you notice squinting, extreme redness, or if your dog is pawing at their eye, the urgency increases to an emergency.
  • Potential Risks: These are signs of pain and potential damage to the eye surface that could lead to vision loss if not treated promptly.

How does taking a photo of my dog's eye help with veterinary triage?

  • Visual Assessment: A clear, high-resolution photo in natural light allows a professional to assess the volume, color, and consistency of the discharge.
  • Tissue Evaluation: It helps the vet evaluate the state of the surrounding tissues, such as the conjunctiva and eyelids.
  • Decision Support: This information helps determine if the situation is a routine infection or an urgent case requiring immediate intervention, potentially saving an unnecessary trip to the emergency clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, a thick green eye discharge, also described as mucopurulent, can be associated with inflammation and prolapse of the gland of the nictitating membrane (cherry eye), a condition common in young dogs and certain breeds. This occurs when the red glandular mass swells and protrudes over the leading margin of the nictitans. Additionally, mucopurulent discharge can result from obstructions of the nasolacrimal duct.

Chapter: Ophthalmology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 498)

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops for my dog's green discharge?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human eye medications on a dog without professional guidance. Some human drops contain ingredients that can be harmful or even cause the eye to worsen if a corneal ulcer is present.

faqs.question: Should I clean the discharge away?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can gently wipe the area with a soft, damp cloth and lukewarm water to keep your dog comfortable. However, this will not cure the underlying infection, which requires veterinary treatment.

faqs.question: Is green discharge always an infection?
faqs.answer: While it is most commonly a sign of a bacterial infection, it can also be caused by a foreign object stuck in the eye or a lack of tear production causing the remaining tears to become thick and mucoid.
